points: Fractals are self-similar geometric shapes where smaller copies of the entire pattern appear at every scale level. They occur naturally in ferns, trees, coastlines, clouds, and mountains. Mathematical fractals include Sierpinski Carpet (R_n = 8^n squares remain at step n), Sierpinski Gasket (equilateral triangle fractal with area → 0), and Koch Snowflake (perimeter → ∞ while area remains finite). Fractals appear in ancient Indian temples (Kandariya Mahadev), African traditional art (Fulani blankets), and modern art (M.C. Escher).

Solids with flat surfaces (polyhedra) are defined by faces (flat surfaces), edges (line segments where faces meet), and vertices (corner points). Key solids include prisms (two parallel congruent bases, n-sided prism has n+2 faces, 3n edges, 2n vertices) and pyramids (one base with triangular lateral faces, n-sided pyramid has n+1 faces, 2n edges, n+1 vertices). A net is a 2D unfolding of a 3D solid; a cube has 11 possible nets. The shortest path between two points on a cuboid's surface can be found by unfolding the cuboid into a net and measuring the straight line between the points.

Orthogonal (perpendicular) projection maps 3D objects onto 2D planes. A line of length l at angle θ projects to length p = l·cos(θ). Parallel lines project to parallel lines (fundamental property). Three-view orthogonal projection uses mutually perpendicular planes: front view (vertical plane, elevation), top view (horizontal plane, plan), and side view (side plane, profile). Together, these three views uniquely represent a 3D object. Isometric projection is a special case where unit distances along three perpendicular axes project to equal lengths; isometric grid paper (with three families of lines at 120° angles) is used for practical isometric drawing in engineering and design applications.
